speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. It's January 24, 1996, and we're interviewing Rena Gelissen. You were telling us about-- you turned yourself in for labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor camp-- to save the lives of the people that-- that were keeping me there for almost a year. And my life, too, at the same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the next-- next morning, we were finished. They brought me to pack my suitcase, told me to wear-- take warm clothes, because it's winter. And I said I'd take nice and warm clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left there. I came to the train station. And at the train station, I saw many Jews there, and didn't see a regular train-- just cattle cars. You know what they look like-- it's a big-- just a little square on the top, no doors or anything to it. And they put a-- a-- something to lead the animals up. They was not for us. They were just-- we were helping each other to just-- to go in and to-- And by the time we ended up, and they closed the cattle cars-- the doors-- there were at least 80 or 90 of us there. There was no toilet facilities. Only a bucket was there. And we were-- nobody could sit down on the suitcase, even. That's how packed we were, really tightly packed. And there was one woman there with a baby in her arms. But that's not so important, I think, to talk about that. And then nobody knew where we were going. And one bucket-- it took quite a while till anybody dared to go to the bucket. I remember a daughter and a mother. It was-- the daughter was keeping her coat open so the mother could go in the bucket. But later on, we didn't pay much attention because the excrement was flowing over the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this took-- I was in a trance in this one. I wasn't thinking anymore. I was just so-- this doesn't look like we are going to a work camp. Not to someone-- not a woman with a little baby. What is she going to do there? So-- so I just didn't even pay attention, didn't even think. And suddenly I heard, is somebody here from Poland? And I listened for a while. I said, yeah, I am Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they're all Slovakian Jews. I am Polish. An we would like to see the signs when we're going by-- we saw from the sides some signs. OK. But if we get it straight-- after they passed by, they couldn't read it. I said, if we lift you up, could you read the signs. I said, I will try. So two men lifted me up to that little square that is in that cattle car. And just iron bars and it's open-- that's all the air we were getting in through little square for animals. And-- and I read [POLISH]. I said, yeah. That's a Polish name. Auschwitz in Polish is [POLISH]. And I spelled it to them-- I said, this is [POLISH]. And this was all of it. And then, just arriving there, and being miserable the whole time-- not-- not even thinking. And coming there-- coming there, so many days later-- I'm not sure if it was four days or it was five days. I think between four and six days, something like this. Because they were stopping-- it isn't so far by distance. But they were stopping to pick up more Jews. And then that we came-- we came in at one morning, 11 o'clock. I think it was a Sunday. I think it was-- we found that it was the 26th. The 22nd I gave myself up. So apparently, it was four days. And they told us to-- prisoners in striped uniforms, men, came, Polish-- Polish men, they were talking German to us. But I found out later that they were Polish. Just jump off-- in German, they said-- the train quickly, because they're going to beat you if you don't, and they're going to beat us, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we don't want to beat you. We're supposed to beat you if you don't go fast enough. So he picked a suitcase up. It's four to five feet, the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were standing up all the time because there's no place to sit down or to lie down. You have to jump down. So it's a miracle that you didn't break both your legs. But with a suitcase, and then a pile of suitcases was already before me-- because I was in the-- one of the last compartments, and they got one of the last cattle cars. So there was a pile of suitcases. I assume they were already out, they were standing and lining up. And I saw confusion, people going here, children there, and grownups there. And-- and-- and the suitcases there are just in a pile.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm a neat person. So I walk up to one-- told me to go there. So I'm going there. But before I lined up with the younger women, where they assigned me, they told me to go there, I walked closer a little bit to an SS man. I said, how are we going to recognize the-- our suitcases afterwards, I said, if it's in a pile? He said, shut up and get in line. So we went in line, and we started marching. This is the first Jewish transport. What we saw when we came in, only 1,000 of us were picked. The rest of them were somewhere. I was hoping the elderly people and children and the younger ones and mothers with the baby, were going to this-- I remember her, a officer asking her-- I think the baby was dead. But he was asking, want to give up the baby? And she said, no. So she went with the elderly people. I said, hopefully, they're going to go somewhere to a warmer place with their parents and with their grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the young girls, 1,000 of us are picked. I'm one of the 1,000. And we marched into camp. And I looked at the gate. Up on the top of the gate, it said [GERMAN]. And because I studied German, I knew what it meant-- which 'work will make-- will make you free.' We marched in. And through the big gate, on the left side are the-- the-- buildings, brick buildings. And on the right side, brick buildings. And in the middle is a wall. It's-- it's a camp road, but it's divided with the wall. It looked like temporary, because half of it was going to the camp, to the men's camp, and half of it on our-- or more on our side, because their entrance was on the other side and ours was this entrance.

speaker: interviewee
text: We entered to a block-- the buildings are called blocks. And this is block five-- block four. Going into block four, we're standing in line. I'm still standing in line, all of us-- in five, we're standing to go in. So the first one started to go in. And the building-- to the back door from the building, coming out this door, in the front from the building. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, we see there-- there are people coming out with bare heads. And they look-- I said, I saw already on the road, these men working without caps. And there were shaved heads. I said, it looks like more. It must be an insane institution or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said, but they took us to work here. And what are those insane people doing with us? I still in my suit, and still fairly decent looking, and with-- with expectations to be treated like a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, one of sister was shouting to the other one, it's me. It's me. And then we found out that that creature there, with the shaved head, and with the Russian uniform with the holes in it, with lice and holes from-- which, I know the things already because later I got the same uniform. And those were our sisters, and-- and our people. So by the time I came-- oh, yeah, and they-- they took away everything from us, my watch and everything. If you have something-- it was raining and snowing, by the way. It's March weather. And they took away everything, the jewelry. If you have any jewelry, don't give it to them-- you have no idea what they did to us. And I don't even want to tell you, the sister said. So throw away everything. I had a chrome watch, not a very expensive one that I had once. So I took the watch off and stepped on it. And otherwise, I had no jewelry. And we were poor-- poor farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked in. And they said-- asked me, where are you from? What nationality? I said, Polish. And the woman was Slovakian-- so she put me down that I was a Gentile, Polish. I'll tell you why later that I'm telling you. So she said, but the other-- the discipline of-- of processing is the same thing as with all the other. She said, take off this and take of this, and put-- I said, how are we going to find out where to find the clothes? Throw it down there and shut up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started-- and then suddenly, I hear shouting. Hey you, with the earrings. I had from my grandfather, he gave all his granddaughters on the first day of school a gold earring with a turquoise stone. She said, take those earrings off! And I was so used to it, wearing just those one earrings, it was part of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took it-- take off those earrings, or I'm going to tear it out of you. So I took off quickly the earring and putting it in a box where the jewelry was. And then we started being processed-- shaved, the heads, and all bodily hair shaved. And after they shaved us, they dunk-- they dunked us. We had to go in in a vat with disinfectant there, which was burning like fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we're naked. We're standing in line. In the meantime, by the time it was my turn, other ones had coming out already, with the Russian uniforms. Already they're processed, they're going this way to go outside. And we are going towards a table. From a distance, I notice there is a table. And I heard a girl say to lie down naked there on the table. And the officer and with rubber gloves, the German officer, was doing something to them. And she was screaming. I had no idea of a gynecological testing or something, they're looking for jewelry up there. I didn't know what they're doing. But she was screaming. And another one was screaming. I said, I have to see to it that I don't make that table. So here is that other line. So I cheated them out of this one, too. That other line going already to the clothes, yeah. From the table, perhaps, it's going to get the clothes. Pardon me. And-- and I slipped in there in the other row, and I didn't get to the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on, I found that they were looking for jewelry-- if they were hiding this, in every crevice in the body.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, I went without it. I went in. I had the uniform. While we were picking the uniforms, we had to exchange with each other long pants. Some of them had no buttons, the Russian uniforms-- hard buttons, big holes from the bullets in it. I didn't know where the bullets, and I didn't know from Russian uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I'm saying Russian uniforms. I didn't know by that time, because there were no insignia on it. I didn't know that they were. I found out later that they were Russian uniforms. Because in '41, Germany attacked Russia, and they took prisoners of war, OK? And we had those uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And no underwear, nothing at all. Just the uniforms with the holes and with the lice in it because there were already for months there, for a whole year, I think. And a piece of wood, for-- different sizes of wood with a leather strip. And that was all the clothes that we got. And with that clothes, we went. After we were processed, everybody was ready. They put, like, 80 of us in-- in one room, or maybe more. We were quite a lot of them. And that was block five, the next-- next building-- next building to it. And we walked in. There was a lot of straw lying on the floor, and nothing else in that room. And some of the girls sat down in those Russian uniforms. And suddenly, bed bugs were jumping all over us. They covered us. And we started screaming and crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was one of the first ones to jump up and pound on the door, because anybody in their right mind would expect something like this, that human beings would do that to you? And I pounded, let us out! Open up! Open up! The door was locked, by the way, and we couldn't go anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the rest of the night, there was-- they were crying and screaming and sleeping. Some of them who got very exhausted slept. I sat up-- I slept part of the time, sat up part of the time. And I was praying and hoping for one thing only-- that my sister Danka would not come, because I knew that I was a lot stronger than she. And whatever's going to happen after this, a pretty good idea that it's not a work camp, or whatever it's going to be-- that nothing decent or nothing humane treatment. And-- and the next morning, we went to the-- they got us out from that block and brought us over to the men's camp and put the tattoo on us. And my tattoo is 1,760-- the 1,000 counts for the 1,000 German woman they had there. From the Jewish women, I'm the 716th there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the first Jewish transport that ever came-- came to Auschwitz, and the first woman transport. Because they didn't have any women before. They had Poles, and they had other people there, but not woman. What kind of work did they have you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, a couple of days, they were waiting for more transports to come. So three days, we weren't working. And the next day after the-- after the tattoo, they put us in-- they put my group of people with me in block 10. Block is the buildings, again. There were 10 buildings. And there was a little fence, a wire fence. And then in the back of this was block 11. And then the other blocks, 14 of them, were for the men on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after we were processed, we had the tattoo, we went to the block, to block 10. And when-- every morning, after roll call-- every morning we had to go on the-- on the street, in the camp road for roll call-- I looked for the new transports coming in. And the second or the third day, there was my sister, Danka. So my nightmare came true, that I knew she was going to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I took her-- she was supposed to go in other buildings. But I took her by the hand, and I said, don't say anything, and pretend that you are just-- she was wearing already the same uniform I was wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to the block elder. It was-- they assigned to us a Jewish girl from Czechoslovakia. And I said, could I have my sister with me here in this block? She said, OK. Somebody else was sleeping with me. There were three layers of bunk beds. And I asked other girl to go to the-- somewhere else, could I keep my sister with me? So I kept my sister with me. She--

speaker: interviewee
text: And a couple of days later, they started-- had roll call. And they didn't tell us anything, but suddenly, I saw forming details. And Danka was standing next to me, because she didn't have, for several days, food. She got some food, and she had diarrhea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked the block elder, would she let her in to go in? And she didn't. And I shook the block elder a little bit. Didn't she have a brother or sister? So I was punished, either way. SS woman walked up to me. She threw me on the ground and she beat me up real good. And we walked-- we marched out to work from Auschwitz I to a detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked with-- slave labor, with lorries-- we sifted sand-- whatever could be sifted, was wet, and it was raining-- and loaded it up on lorries. I don't know if you know what a lorry is-- iron things on wheels, which is supposed to go on railroad-- railroad tracks. But we were pushing them, four on one side, four on the other side. And you had to watch out not to lose that shoe that was on you with a strap, because you'd be punished, they said, if you lose that. So if-- I don't know what happened to once they lost it. I didn't lose mine. And pushing up the lorry, emptying the sand, coming back, and sifting. And a couple of days or a week later, we were-- they told us to carry bricks from one place to another one, and-- about 10 bricks like this. And then it wasn't enough with us. And I had Danka always with me, OK, standing next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and they weren't satisfied with this. So they made, for-- for their pleasure-- the SS guard with us, there was a kapo with us, is a German woman. I don't know. Do you understand what a kapo is?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was brought from a prison from Germany, serving a sentence for whatever reason. There are different, three categories. But that's maybe not important, to mention the categories. And she's a kapo. And-- and she's going to stay in camp. She's German, [GERMAN]. It means she's Aryan. And she-- they stay in different buildings than we, already in Auschwitz I. They-- they already had different buildings. And they had pillows, I think, and sheets and blankets. And we had just a straw-- straw-filled burlap sack and those beds, those bunk beds, and a thin blanket, like a rag over it. But anyway, we're still together. And then they made us throw-- instead of carrying the blocks, they put us six to 10 feet apart, and throwing the blocks. So you have to receive it with your hand till your hands were bleeding. And that was lasting till about September, I think. And then they-- one morning, we were lining up on roll call. Nobody ever told us. We were just, like-- maybe they-- in the free world, you tell an animal more. Some are directed, you're going this way. Just marched out, and we kept on marching. Not till they felt we marched the other direction, then-- and the field before, then the detail. And in that other direction, we discovered when we came in, there were barracks there-- looked like stables. They were brick, too, but practically no windows there in it. And we marched in and assigned to different barracks. And in the barracks were three-- three shelves. And a wall here and a wall here. And the shelves, wooden planks, and a little rag of a blanket there. And we started out with about six or so on one of those planks-- six on one, six on the second, six on the other one. But then every single day, one more transport-- they march us out for details to work with the same kapo. The kapo moved to Auschwitz, too. This was the men's camp, and they didn't have the other buildings ready. So that's why we were in the men's camp, and they divided it with that wall. Then they took the wall away, and if remained a men's camp. And block 11 was the block of death. That's where they were making all the executions. While I was still in block 10, I heard shooting through the window. I was just on this bunk bed, where the window was. And the window was boarded up. This was on the second floor. It was boarded up. And I looked through the-- to the-- openings between the window, and I saw on the wall opposite me, I saw soldiers standing in green uniforms, holding their hands like this. And then I heard the bullet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans were standing under my window. I couldn't see them, the SS. But I saw the soldiers there. And I discovered-- I think, these must be the uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked later on, and the-- through the window-- shouted to him. And he threw me a note. And he said, yes, that was. 12,000 of them were here. 7,000 are already dead, and you are wearing their uniforms. And 5,000 are particularly in block 11, shooting them every single day and killing them. That's how I found out. And then we went to Birkenau. And then in Birkenau were those shelves. And we were six first, but so many transports were coming in every single day, we ended up with 12 of us, instead of in the length, hanging the-- feet hanging down, and just-- then just with a rag of a blanket. And this is Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have an idea of when exactly you arrived in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- we came in in March. And this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was September-- either September or October. September or October, the same year. So we were from March-- March, April, May. About five to six months we were in Auschwitz I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what year are we talking, now that you got to Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. We're still talking '42. Because we came in March, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to make sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. Only it is October now, we ended up in Birkenau. And in Birkenau was the same thing, details going out, only a lot harsher-- the sleeping quarters, and the beatings and things was getting-- they were more cruel and more experienced every time more transports came. Because they were using us for slave labor. I don't know how useful it was, because those bricks throwing wasn't a useful job. So I said, they're just waiting for us to die off, and then other ones. And they weren't very careful with which one they killed or whoever they killed, because they could be killed by a kapo. He could be killed by the SS guard that was with us. We marched out with a kapo that didn't have-- that was wearing a striped dress like we were. By the way, we got-- at one point in Birkenau, they took away the Russian uniforms, and they changed us to burlap dresses, a long dress with long sleeves and buttoned up all along. And that-- for that year, the end of this year, the whole winter, we were just in that dress without underwear, without anything, and just in those wooden shoes-- we were working, again, in the details, the same as the other ones before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to get into any other detail? Any work detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't try to get in because, first place, I was Polish Jew, and I didn't have any relatives or family. All the Jews there, the Jewish girls that had positions were the first ones, the Slovakian. And the Slovakian girls had their cousins, had their sisters, had their-- and they gave them the positions. And even if I wanted to, and I could have to, I didn't want for another reason-- they don't give you a position with two. They're not going to let my sister go out alone. She's never going to-- she went out once alone during the whole stay in Birkenau, until we went somewhere else. And she was beaten up almost half dead, came back to march, and I thought for sure that at night from the details that were carrying the dead, the ones that they shot or they killed or the dogs tore them apart-- the girls, had to carry them in. Carry in the dead ones. And I was looking, the whole time, while they were marching, and I saw-- every one of them, I saw on the shoulders, two shoulders, two girls carrying, I thought it was my sister. But it wasn't. She was beaten up because she was late. They let them go to the latrine before marching into camp, and she was the last one coming out. I wasn't there to hold her hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she wasn't very alert in camp. OK-- she-- she was depending on me. That one day was a coincidence, unpleasant. One detail that isn't important.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she marched out with another commando-- commandos, they were called, another detail-- and wasn't with me. So she had a beating. Otherwise, I took the beatings, usually, protecting her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were jobs assigned? Were you put in details? Were you able to line up in certain detail-- how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a roll call. Say we are now in Birkenau, and there are hundreds of us there. We're standing in rows of five like this. They're counting us in the morning. Roll call is 4 o'clock in the morning. The block elder and the room elder-- every block elder had an assistant called a room elder. They are using sticks to get you off the bed if you can't wake up. And they're beating you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is another reason that I didn't want to be a block elder, or a room elder-- I couldn't do that. So I wasn't going to be. I wasn't beaten very often because I was alert and awake. And the minute I woke up, I pulled my sister down, and she was next to me. So everything that I could. I remembered the words that I promised Mama, take care of your younger sister. Take care of [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did as much. And she cooperated very nicely with everything. And being that she was the youngest child and spoiled and babied and favored, I'm amazed how she could go through everything. And whatever happened-- scabies, or later on malaria-- she had it. I didn't have the scabies. I had a little bit itching on fingers. She had it on her whole body. And I had to sell the portion of bread to get some salve to save her.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, later on, they were choosing-- in Auschwitz, they were choosing, Mengele was mostly the one choosing for different details. OK? And I had- I knew only that Mengele had details, different kinds. He was choosing for other jobs, too, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- what I--and I saw people, while we were marching out, other people, other girls, like us, in the same-- and he had the white apron with them, wearing, too. Were going somewhere, doing something under the SS and under the kapo, doing something. But they looked terrible. They were skinny, and their eyes were dead, just practically holes looking at it when we passed them by. And then I asked in the camp-- you see, this-- we didn't associate with-- with anybody, because you came in at 7 o'clock from-- you stop from work. Three hours, roll call. By the time you had the little piece of bread, and a bowl of tea, plain tea, and drank it, you'd throw yourself on the thing-- on the plank, and you fell asleep. There was no time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they later on, sometimes, if they had information, they would tell us something-- the block elder, the room elder. But not very often. If they did, that's mostly to the Slovakian girls. So we were a little bit isolated, Danka and I, because we were from Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the way, I did not get-- they have the Star of David on my tattoo. I had a tattoo, as you can see the scar from where my number was removed. It's right here. This one dot is left. A Jewish doctor removed it. And he said he didn't want to cut it in crooked. He wanted a straight line, and that-- nobody's going to bother me about it. He offered to do it for me. And-- and every Jewish girl there, or everybody in camp, all the Jews, had to have a-- had a triangle, half of a Jew star, the triangle. And on their number, too, was a triangle. And I didn't have the triangle. I didn't have here. So I could have slept with the Gentiles in the other block, if my sister wasn't there and have better life, maybe, or something. But I probably wouldn't have done it anyway. But anyway, this is what it was, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about Mengele looking for different people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele choosing. So that one day, Mengele is choosing. And-- but I have no connection between Mengele and these people that I saw there. I have no idea who they are. So he's choosing 100 from-- roll call is over. And he said, you, you, you. 100 of them. From 100, he chose 50. And-- and Danka was not among these 50. So I sold my portion of bread to one of the other kapos that I knew-- the kapo that was ours, her friend. So I-- she saw me every day marching out with Emma, the other one. Erika was her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went and talked to Erika. Erika had the list. I said, do you have the list for those 50 people that we had chosen now? She said yes. I said, I'm very close with my sister. And I take care of her. She's my younger sister. Could you give me-- could you scratch out somebody else's name and put me in? My sister in? And here's my portion of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave her the portion of bread. She scratched out the other name. The girl went, and Danka came in. A couple of days later, it's quarantine. And the quarantine, they gave us clean dresses again, and the white aprons. And a couple of days later-- like five, six days later-- we march out, 50 of us march out. But Danka is with me, so I'm not so worried, and I didn't pay attention to really what was going on. And while we are lined up, this is the roll call-- the camp road. And opposite the camp road was a little building, just one-room building, a brick building. And that was Mengele's office. Because when we came over there, we suddenly had to go into the office. And there was a scribe there taking the names and the numbers and writing them down, at least I think so. All of them, all 50 of us. And Mengele wasn't there. And he had a SS. On this side is roll call. And we are on the other side, just the 50 separated. And there are three or four SS counting the people here. Nobody's counting us. We're just standing. And suddenly, somebody comes out from behind the building, Mengele's building, with a sheet of paper and a pencil. And anybody who had a sheet of paper and a pencil, they're walking around while everybody else was roll call, was somebody, a prominent-- either a block elder or had some function more than just the plain people, like I was and Danka, working in the field outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see her pointing at-- at somebody that she knew. And she called her. And I saw her getting in the paper, and scratching something out-- scratching her name out, and took her away, went behind the block. And I said to Danka, I said, guess what? I said, there were some of them that go to-- there were some rumors that Mengele is doing sterilization and all kinds of other experiments. And-- and she took out this one. And she's a prominent person.

speaker: interviewee
text: If she knew we were happy to be in that detail because maybe finally, this is already so many months later-- and another winter is coming. We're gonna be working outside. So we were happy we were gonna be maybe somewhere under a roof, maybe in a sewing room or somewhere, in a better-- in a better detail. And I said, this could not be a good detail. And before that day, I-- Danka was very-- getting very depressed. And-- and she sort of-- even that little piece of bread she almost didn't want to chew up. And I was getting worried about it. And I woke her up one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, Danka, what-- what is it that you are acting differently than you did at the beginning? What can you act here? Then it's not worth living here. She said, this is not life. And I'm gonna be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, then I'm in the same boat with you. No, you're not. You look a lot better than I. And if they're going to be chosen for that, they're going to choose me and not going to choose you. And I'm not afraid to die alone. I don't want you-- I-- I would be glad if you're alive, but I'm worried only about the trip to the gas chambers. I see how, after the selections-- by the way, in the meantime, selections are going on. They're choosing the weaker ones, what they think is weaker-- just putting it on this way. That was all life was worth. And they're throwing out on flatbeds, throwing up those that are chosen to die. And she said, I saw them, how throw them up on the flatbeds. And it's horrible, just like rotten potatoes. That's the way they treat them. She said, and I'm afraid of that. I said, OK. You give me your hand. I said, here's a holy book. And God is everywhere, I said. And Mom and Dad are standing right here. I swear to you. If I'm going to be chosen, I'll go with you. You're not going to be alone. We're going to die together. From that day on, she-- she just was a different person. She wanted to live again and stay with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's take a break here and change tape.
